coredata.dc:description: Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Vp) and Vibrio harveyi (Vh) are two pathogenic vibriosis causing agents which are prevalent in commercial aquaculture practices. In spite of the increasing Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in pathogenic organisms due to inappropriate usage of conventional antibiotics, there is an urgent need of alternative therapeutic antimicrobials. Metallic nanoparticles, particularly silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), are considered effective antimicrobials against a wide spectrum of microorganisms. Tunable properties such as size, shape, and surface functionalization enhance the properties and activities of AgNPs. Studies have explored the use of carbon dots (CDs) as an alternative source for reducing and capping AgNPs, given the limitations of using plant extracts as reducing agents for AgNP synthesis. Most of the studies synthesized CDs using various chemical precursors; however, plant-derived or biomass-derived CDs offer several advantages, such as biocompatibility, easy availability of precursors, renewability, hydrophilicity, and no requirement of heteroatom doping. In the present study, an eco-friendly hydrothermal technique was employed to synthesize high fluorescent, Biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) from the leaves of two medicinal plants, Aegle marmelos (AM) and Euphorbia hitra (EH). These CDs were then used as reducing agents for the green synthesis of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The absorbance peaks of synthesized AM CDs and EH CDs were at 267 nm and 274 nm, indicating π–π* electronic transitions. Upon adding CDs to AgNO3 led to a decline in CD absorption peaks and the emergence of AgNP SPR peaks at 467 nm and 479 nm, confirming AgNP formation. AM CDs showed a narrow peak at 530 nm (excitation at 360 nm), while EH CDs had a broad peak at 560 nm and AM CDs exhibited a narrow peak at 530 nm. AgNP@CD NCs did not emit luminescence, likely due to quenching during the AgNPs reduction. FTIR identified O-H, C≡C, and N-H functional groups in CDs. AgNP@AM CD NCs showed minor vibrational signals, while AgNP@EH CD NCs showed a complete reduction. The average particle sizes of the synthesized AgNPs were 8.51 nm and 23.59 nm for AM and EH, respectively. The synthesized AgNP@CD nanocomposites showed effective antivibriocidal activity with the lowest Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of 3.9 μg/mL on Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Vp) by EH AgNP@CD NCs. The in vitro antioxidant studies revealed effective radical scavenging activity with the lowest IC50 value of 5.29 μg/mL on DPPH nitrogen free radical by AM AgNP@CD NCs. The present study found effective antivibriocidal and antioxidant properties of biomass derived AgNP@CD NCs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Vp) and Vibrio harveyi (Vh) are two pathogenic vibriosis causing agents which are prevalent in commercial aquaculture practices. In spite of the increasing Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in pathogenic organisms due to inappropriate usage of conventional antibiotics, there is an urgent need of alternative therapeutic antimicrobials. Metallic nanoparticles, particularly silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), are considered effective antimicrobials against a wide spectrum of microorganisms. Tunable properties such as size, shape, and surface functionalization enhance the properties and activities of AgNPs. Studies have explored the use of carbon dots (CDs) as an alternative source for reducing and capping AgNPs, given the limitations of using plant extracts as reducing agents for AgNP synthesis. Most of the studies synthesized CDs using various chemical precursors; however, plant-derived or biomass-derived CDs offer several advantages, such as biocompatibility, easy availability of precursors, renewability, hydrophilicity, and no requirement of heteroatom doping. In the present study, an eco-friendly hydrothermal technique was employed to synthesize high fluorescent, Biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) from the leaves of two medicinal plants, Aegle marmelos (AM) and Euphorbia hitra (EH). These CDs were then used as reducing agents for the green synthesis of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The absorbance peaks of synthesized AM CDs and EH CDs were at 267 nm and 274 nm, indicating π–π* electronic transitions. Upon adding CDs to AgNO3 led to a decline in CD absorption peaks and the emergence of AgNP SPR peaks at 467 nm and 479 nm, confirming AgNP formation. AM CDs showed a narrow peak at 530 nm (excitation at 360 nm), while EH CDs had a broad peak at 560 nm and AM CDs exhibited a narrow peak at 530 nm. AgNP@CD NCs did not emit luminescence, likely due to quenching during the AgNPs reduction. FTIR identified O-H, C≡C, and N-H functional groups in CDs. AgNP@AM CD NCs showed minor vibrational signals, while AgNP@EH CD NCs showed a complete reduction. The average particle sizes of the synthesized AgNPs were 8.51 nm and 23.59 nm for AM and EH, respectively. The synthesized AgNP@CD nanocomposites showed effective antivibriocidal activity with the lowest Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of 3.9 μg/mL on Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Vp) by EH AgNP@CD NCs. The in vitro antioxidant studies revealed effective radical scavenging activity with the lowest IC50 value of 5.29 μg/mL on DPPH nitrogen free radical by AM AgNP@CD NCs. The present study found effective antivibriocidal and antioxidant properties of biomass derived AgNP@CD NCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Vibrio is a gram negative bacteria belongs to the Vibrionaceae family comprise of 65 well documented species and are seen in various aquatic environments from rivers, estuaries, seas to deep-oceanic waters [1]. In aquatic environments, vibrio bacteria are microbial indicators because they are non-culturable in winter and are detectable in warmer temperatures as well as during reduced rainfall (summer) [2]. First reported vibrio infection in humans is in fifth century BCE as an acute septicemia by Hippocrates, Greek physician [3]. Vibriosis is a local and more general septicemia infection caused by vibrio bacteria often regarded as an opportunistic pathogen that infects wide range of aquatic, terrestrial non-human animals and humans [4]. In commercial aquaculture practices, the outbreak of vibriosis is more frequent, lethal and cause substantial economic loss [5]. Vibrio parahaemolyticus (Vp) and Vibrio harveyi (Vh) are quite prevalent vibrio species in aquaculture practices affecting both fin and shell fish and cause acute hepatopancreatic necrosis disease (AHPND) and bacterial white tail disease respectively. These species can also transmit to humans through uncooked or partial cooked aquatic organisms [6]. To overcome the disease outbreaks, the inappropriate usage of antimicrobials as therapeutics and prophylactics brings out the evolution of antimicrobial resistant strains that cannot be cured in cultured organisms or humans [7].

In recent decades, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) shown effective antimicrobial properties to combat the multidrug resistance problem and considered as viable alternative therapeutics to antibiotics [8,9]. The synthesis of AgNPs is extensively studied using various physical and chemical synthesis methods, but these methods are expensive and toxic to the environment and associated life. An eco-friendly green synthesis process for the synthesis of NPs that is reliable, facile, and biocompatible can overcome the challenges associated with these methods [10,11]. Numerous studies have reported the surface passivation or doping of AgNPs using various biomaterials including plant phytochemicals, microbial secondary metabolites, carbon dots (CDs) shows synergistic antimicrobial properties [12,13]. Tunable shape and size are considered as the key properties of nanoparticles. Various studies have been carried out with green synthesis of plant and microbial extracts which showed heterogeneous particle size and shapes [14]. The homogeneous shape and size of nanoparticles can also be achieved by combining the AgNPs with CDs, a novel type of zero dimensional nanomaterials with size less than < 10 nm and have metal-free fluorescent property [15]. Among the various types, metal-based and carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit exceptional optical characteristics with several fascinating aspects [16].

AgNPs and CD nanocomposites exhibit electrostatic interactions with microorganism surface proteins and incorporate into the cellular membranes eventually leads to cell destruction. Biomass derived CDs are advantageous over CDs derived from chemical precursors. Biomass derived CDs are ultrafine size, biocompatible, hydrophilic, low toxic, and does not require surface passivation with other hetero atoms [15,17,18]. Apart from these properties, CDs are sustainable reducing agents for noble metal nanoparticle synthesis and effective than plant and microbial metabolites [19]. The shielding of CDs on AgNPs make them more stable than the AgNPs synthesized with other reducing agents [20].

Two significant medicinal plant leaves were selected as biomass for the synthesis of CDs: Aegle marmelos and Euphorbia hitra Linn in this study. Aegle marmelos (AM) of Rutaceae family have been used in Ayurveda to treat various human ailments and commonly called as Indian Bael or Indian Quince [21]. Euphorbia hitra Linn (EH) commonly known as asthma plant belongs to Euphorbiaceae family. Every part of these two plants is known for diverse pharmacological properties [22]. Previous studies reported the bioactive compounds isolated from both AM and EH as well as their respective pharmacological properties [21,23], are shown in Table 1 .These two perennial plants are highly prevalent in the Indian subcontinent. Besides their distribution and pharmacological properties, plant parts such as leaves, flowers, roots, bark, seeds, etc. are natural resources and precursor molecules for the synthesis of CDs. Plant leaves can synthesize CDs without requiring the removal of the entire plant, allowing for large-scale production [24].

The wide range of pharmacological properties, composition, and easy availability of these two plants inspired the present study to utilize them as precursors for the synthesis of CDs. The objectives of the current study are (1) Hydrothermal assisted synthesis of CDs from leaves of AM and EH (2) Synthesis of Ag@CD nanocomposites (3) Evaluation of optical and morphological properties of Ag@CD nanocomposites and (4) A comparative anti-vibriocidal and in vitro antioxidant properties of Ag@CD nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-pot green synthesis of CDs using hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The leaves of AM and EH plants collected from Rajamahendravaram, Andhra Pradesh and were confirmed by expert, Dr. A. Matta Reddy, School of life and health sciences, Adikavi Nannaya University, Rajamahendravaram. The pulverized plant powders of AM and EH were subjected to synthesis of carbon dots using one pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2 % of AM & EH powders were suspended in DH2O separately and kept on magnetic stirring at 100 °C for 1 h to achieve homogeneous distribution of particles. The solution was placed in Hydrothermal autoclave (Teflon coated) and heated it at 180 °C for 16 h. After cooling, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove bulky particles. The obtained supernatant was filtered with Whatman No.1 filter paper followed by purification of CDs using 0.22 µm PVDF membrane syringe filters and stored at 4 °C until further use [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: One pot synthesis of AgNP@CD nanocomposites (NCs)
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL of AM & EH CDs were slowly released to the 10 mM of 90 mL of AgNO3 separately under constant stirring at 70 °C. The solution colour changes from pale yellow to dark brown through time, which confirmed the reduction of Ag+ ions to Ago. The mixture was further centrifuged and pellet was washed with 70 % ethyl alcohol thrice and dried at 70 °C [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and morphological properties of AgNP@CD NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance of AM & EH CD’s and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) of AgNP@CD NC’s were confirmed using UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 2600) at wavelengths spanning from 200 to 800 nm. The functional groups of AM & EH CDs and AgNP@CD NC’s are identified by using Fourier Transformed Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) (Bruker Alpha II) over the range of 500 cm− 1 to 4000 cm− 1. The crystalline nature and average crystalline size of AgNP@CD NC’s were evaluated by Powder X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku 600) with monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (K = 1.5406 Ao) and it was operated at 30 mA and 40 KV from 10 to 90° in 2θ angles. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR TEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific F200) analysis was performed to determine the AgNP@CD NC’s particle size distribution and morphology at 200 kV. The Field Emission Scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Carl zeiss SUPRA-55) coupled with Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to analyse the shape and elemental compositions of AgNP@CD NC’s.
The anti-vibriocidal of AgNP@CD NC’s were evaluated using agar well diffusion and Dilution micro broth (DMB) assays according to the Pangi VN et.al 2023 [26].

The anti-vibriocidal activity of AgNP@CD NCs were evaluated on two-gram negative species, Vibrio parahaemolyticus (MTCC 451) and Vibrio harveyi (ATCC 14126). Muller Hinton Agar (MHA) was inoculated with 105 CFU mL−1 of each bacterial broth and 6 mm (approximately) of wells were made using gel puncture on agar plate. The various concentrations (500–2000 µg mL−1) of AgNP@CD NCs dissolved in 5 % DMSO were added to each well. Streptomycin (200 µg mL−1) was used as positive control and 5 % DMSO was used negative control. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and the zone of inhibitions were calculated.

The Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of AgNP@CD NCs against Vibrio parahaemolyticus and Vibrio harveyi were evaluated using DMB and 96 well micro titre plate. Approximately 105 CFU mL−1 of each bacterial culture was inoculated in Luria Bertani Broth (LB Broth) and the AgNP@CD NCs were added using two fold dilution method with concentrations of 1000–3.9 µg mL−1 and 1 % TTC solution was added into all the wells. Positive and negative controls were maintained. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h of micro titre plates, the MIC was calculated by visual observing the appearance of pink or red color, which indicates growth of the bacteria.
The micro titre plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and the MIC was calculated by visual observation (Appearance of pink or red color indicates growth of the bacteria).
The free radical scavenging activity and ferric reducing activity of AgNP@CD NCs were evaluated to determine their antioxidant properties [27,28].

The free radical scavenging activity of AgNP@CD NCs was determined by using 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay. Approximately 3 mM of DPPH in methanol was added to the samples with various concentrations (40–200 µg mL−1) and incubated in dark for 1 h. The decrease in absorbance values with control was read at 517 nm. The DPPH with methanol was used as control and ascorbic acid was used as standard.

The FRAP with acetate buffer was control and acetate buffer as blank, the reaction mixture was incubated in dark at room temperature for about 4 hrs and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. The FRAP reagent with acetate buffer (pH 3.6) and ascorbic acid were used as control and standard reference respectively. Most studies have been carried out to synthesize metallic NPs using plant and microbial extracts, majority of these extraction processes retain primary metabolites and secondary metabolites with null pharmacological properties may also act as capping agents on NPs. The hindrance effect of these compounds restricts the interaction between reactants and active sites of NPs result in their inefficiency. To mitigate this problem, NPs reduced with CDs may produce surfactant-free NPs and have superior features [29]. Hydrothermal assisted synthesis of CDs is an eco-friendly process that does not require the use of organic solvents. Additional surface passivation is not necessary to enhance safety and reduce toxicity on the surface of CDs. This method for the synthesis of CDs is effective than various high energy consuming methods [30]. The hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups of CDs are responsible for the reduction of Ag+ ions to Ag0. The reduced silver atoms undergo nucleation to form small clusters that grow into NPs. The steric and electrostatic interactions between Ag+ ions and CDs facilitate NP stabilization [13]. In the present study, The CDs of AM and EH plant leaves were synthesized using hydrothermal assisted method and both CDs were used as reducing agents for the AgNPs synthesis separately. First, the obtained CDs and AgNP@CD NCs were subjected to evaluate their optical and morphological properties using various analytical techniques.

The optical properties include absorbance, SPR and surface functional groups of CDs as well as AgNP@CD NCs were evaluated using UV–vis spectroscopy and FTIR. The fluorescence excitation and emission of CDs evaluated using fluorescence spectroscopy.

The absorbance of synthesized AM CDs & EH CDs found absorption peaks at 267 nm and 274 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The resulting π – π* electronic transitions corresponds to the structural patterns of the CDs [31]. The addition of these CDs to the AgNO3 solution during reduction reaction showed a sudden declination in intensity of absorption peaks of CDs and emergence of the SPR of AgNPs. The SPR of AgNP@CD NCs was observed at 467 nm and 479 nm confirming the successful formation of AgNPs reduced by AM & EH CDs (Fig. 1b). The resonance reaction of outermost free electrons on AgNPs causes SPR, which is typically observed for metallic NPs. Similar investigations have shown that the SPR of AgNP@CD NCs was measured at 425 nm [14] and 400 nm [15]. The SPR peak appearance of AgNPs at different wavelength regions can be related to various factors such as the concentration of AgNO3, reducing agent, reaction time, temperature etc. [32–34].

The band gap energies of both AM and EH CDs were calculated using UV–Vis spectroscopy data, demonstrating significant differences between direct and indirect band gap transitions represented in Fig. 2 . The calculated AM CDs direct band gap energy was 3.81 eV, and the indirect band gap was found to be 3.75 eV. whereas, the EH CDs showed a direct band gap of 4.30 eV and an indirect band gap measuring 3.40 eV. EH CDs have a slightly broader direct band gap and a narrower indirect band gap thanAM CDs in the present study. Upon integrating these CDs onto AgNPs, additional alterations wereobserved in the band gap energies. The direct band gap of AM AgNP@CD NCs increased to 3.43 eV, while the indirect band gap increased to 3.35 eV, suggesting a substantial improvement in both transitions. The EH AgNP@CD NCs exhibited a 4.73 eV direct band gap and a 4.56 eV indirect band gap. The shifts observed indicate that the combination of AgNPs and CDs not only increases the total energy gap but also affects the electrical and magnetic properties of the nanocomposites. The observed enhancement in both direct and indirect band gaps following the synthesis of nanocomposites shows the potential of these materials in applications that necessitate high energy band gaps, such as in optoelectronic devices and photocatalysis.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a) the functional groups of AM and EH CDs were identified using FTIR ranging from 500-4000 cm−1. Similar vibrational signals were observed in both samples. The major vibrational signals of CDs observed at 3335 and 3331 cm−1 represents the presence of O-H stretching. Low intensity vibrational signals at 2109 and 2107 cm−1 corresponds to C≡C stretching, and another prominent peak at 1635 cm−1 corresponds to N-H bending functional group vibrations. However, the AM AgNP@CD NCs showed minor vibrational signals and surprisingly complete decrease in the vibrational signals of EH AgNP@ CD NCs (Fig. 3(b)). The minor vibrational stretching shown in inset of Fig. 3 is between 500–700 cm−1 represents the Ag-O bond which confirms the formation of AgNP@CD NCs. The contradictory results were observed by previous studies [14,35].

Hydrothermally synthesized CDs formation follows hypothetically 3 major steps (i) Hydrolysis and Condensation (ii) Self polymerization and (iii) Transformation to core and surface functionalization. The surface functional groups of CDs facilitate a multistep mechanism in the formation of AgNP@CD NCs. In the present study, the FTIR analysis of both AM and EH CDs revealed the presence of three functional groups: O-H, C-C, and N-H, all of which act as reducing agents. Out of these three functional groups, O-H and C≡C are mild reducing agents, and N-H is considered a strong reducing agent with enhanced localized reduction for Ag+ ions. In addition to their reducing activity, CDs also serve as capping agents for the metal ions, ensuring steric stability and preventing NP agglomeration. Fig. 3(c) depicts the hypothetical mechanism of AgNP@CD NCs formation. The intensities of the vibrational signals of AgNP@CD NCs are low when compared to the vibrational signals of CDs. This suggests a stronger interaction between CDs and Ag + ions, possibly due to the higher accessibility of functional groups observed in EH AgNP@CD NCs. Therefore, the surface chemistry of reducing agents is a key factor in the formation of NPs and their stabilization [36].

The fluorescence excitation and emission of AM and EH CDs were represented in Fig. 4 (a and b). Using UV trans illuminator, the insets of Fig. 4(a and b) represent the respective CDs at day light (left) and UV light (365 nm) (right). The AM CDs exhibited narrow and symmetrical peak at 530 nm (Excitation at 360 nm), while EH CDs exhibited broad emission peak at 560 nm. AgNP@CD NCs doesn’t emit any luminescence in this work, this could be due to quenching process of CDs during AgNPs reduction reaction (electron transfer mechanism) [37,38].

The phase analysis of synthesized AgNP@CD NCs was further confirmed using powder XRD method. The obtained raw data was then compared with standard ICDD data with card ICDD No: 03–065-2871. The X-ray diffractogram of AM AgNP@CD and EH AgNP@CD NCs represented four sharp intense peaks with at 2θ of 38.200, 44.400, 64.600, 77.600 and 38.110, 44.220, 64.440, 77.390, respectively. These peaks are attributed to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) planes of face cubic centered (fcc) lattice of the Ag’s with good crystallinity as shown in Fig. 5 (a and b).
The calculated average crystalline size of the AM AgNP@CD and EH AgNP@CD NCs were 13.7 and 15.7 nm using Scherrer formula.

D=.
K λ
β
2 θ
c o s θ

Where λ is the wavelength of the incident X-rays (1.5406 A0), θ is the diffraction angle, β(2θ) being the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the observed diffraction peak in radians, the Scherrer equation K is 0.9 and D is the average diameter of the crystalline, respectively.

CDs not only facilitate synthesis of NPs, but they also play a critical role in modulating the van der Waals force. Minimizing these forces results in stable and well-dispersed NPs [39]. The X-ray diffractogram in the present study, supporting the modulation of van der Waals forces and well-dispersed NCs, can also be observed from HRTEM micrographs. No significant variations were observed in the diffraction angles of both samples, clearly indicating that, irrespective of the precursor of CDs, they exhibit similar efficacy in the reduction of Ag+ ions.
The particle size distribution, surface morphology, elemental mapping of AgNP@CD NCs was evaluated using HRTEM and FESEM-EDX techniques.

The SAED pattern of two types of NCs Fig. 6 (a and e) showing sharp and partially circular rings corresponding to monocrystalline nature of AgNP@CD NCs. The size distribution histogram of two types of NCs depicted in Fig. 6(b and f), The AM AgNP@CD NCs showed size distribution ranging from 0 to 30 nm and EH AgNP@CD NCs showed size distribution ranging from 0 to 60 nm, whereas the average particle sizes are 8.51 and 23.59 nm respectively. The fine fringe pattern of AgNPs was evaluated using ImageJ software and found 0.22 and 0.29 nm of two types of NCs respectively. The amorphous and low contrast of AgNPs prevents the appearance of CDs on their surface, while the high crystallinity of AgNPs is the cause for the bright spots.

The surface morphology and elemental mapping of two AgNP@CD NCs shown in Fig. 7 (a–d). The result revealed AgNPs are spherical, non-homogeneous and relatively uniform in shape and slight agglomeration was observed. However, each agglomerate was composed of small crystallites. The EDAX spectrum shows a strong intense peak for Ag at 3 KeV and minor peaks for Carbon and Oxygen at 0.25 and 0.5 KeV, respectively. The weight % of Ag was 61.83 % and 60.73 % for AM AgNP@CD NCs and EH AgNP@CD NCs respectively. Living organisms are made up of both macronutrients and micronutrients; specifically, plants are rich in micronutrients. Fig. 7(b) and (d) show the presence of elements in traces, such as Ca, Mg, Al, Si, Cl, Na, K, etc., which are constituents of plants. During purification steps, these elements might be filtered along with the CDs, and the appearance of these elements in AgNP@CD NCs may be considered as an impurity, and future methods may be developed to purify the carbon dots without any impurities.

Temperature during nanomaterial synthesis has a profound effect on their morphology and size distribution. Increased temperature during synthesis typically increases the reaction kinetics, resulting in a faster rate of nucleation and growth of NCs. However, in many experimental conditions, higher temperatures lead to domination of growth over nucleation and might form larger NPs and potentially different morphological patterns [40]. To minimize energy consumption and maintain green chemistry principles, the NCs were prepared at 70 °C, which can consume less energy.

The present study carefully differentiated between the observations made using physical and chemical characterization techniques to better understand the properties of green AgNP@CD NCs. Physical techniques, such as HRTEM, FESEM and XRD were confirmed the mono crystalline nature, spherical shape and size distribution ranges between 0–60 nm. The chemical techniques such as UV–Vis and FTIR provided insights into the surface chemistry and functional groups present on the NCs. Particle size of NCs plays a significant role in determining electrostatic interactions, which are key to the stability, aggregation behaviour, and biological activity of the NCs.

Antimicrobials, or antibiotics, are employed in commercial aquaculture to prevent or treat a variety of microbial ailments in cultured organisms. The frequent use of these antimicrobials leads to the emergence of antimicrobial resistance, a great global threat necessitating the development of novel treatments. Antibiotics have a key drawback in that they lack broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties [41]. Recently, AgNPs surface passivated with CDs have drawn great attention to the researchers due to their better stability and efficient antimicrobial properties [15]. AM AgNP@CD NCs demonstrated an effective zone of inhibition on Vp and Vh of 25.3 ± 0.47 mm and 31 ± 0.81 mm, respectively, while EH AgNP@CD NCs showed a ZOI of 17.6 ± 0.47 mm and 18.3 ± 0.47 mm at concentration of 2000 μg mL−1. The concentration dependent ZOI values of two samples on both bacteria are represented in Fig. 8 . The MIC of AM and EH AgNP@CD NCs on Vp are about 7.812 ± 0 and 3.906 ± 0 μg mL−1 respectively. The calculated MIC of two samples for Vh are 7.812 ± 0 μg mL−1. The CDs alone had no antibacterial effect, when CDs fabricated with AgNPs and they release silver ions (Ag+) when in contact with biological fluids or microbial surfaces. These ions can disrupt microbial cell membranes, inhibit enzyme activity, and interfere with DNA replication, leading to microbial death [42]. AgNPs can directly interact with microbial DNA, causing strand breaks, cross-linking, and other forms of damage [43]. This interferes with DNA replication and repair mechanisms, leading to microbial cell death [44] Due to this activity the zone of inhibition is formed. Microorganisms can develop mechanisms to reduce the uptake of AgNPs, such as altering their membrane permeability or efflux pumps to expel AgNPs from the cell. Some microorganisms can produce enzymes or molecules that detoxify silver ions, reducing their antimicrobial effects [18]. The antimicrobial activity of AgNPs@S,N-CQDs showed MIC values which are lower than the present study [15].

The antioxidant potential of AgNP@CD NCs was evaluated using DPPH and FRAP assays and the concentration dependent antioxidant activity of both samples is depicted in Fig. 9 . There is no observed large-scale variations in % of activity among two samples across both antioxidant assays. At a concentration of 200 µg mL−1, AM AgNP@CD NCs demonstrated 83.1 % free radical scavenging activity and 81.3 % ferric reducing activity. In comparison, EH AgNP@CD NCs exhibited 86.2 % free radical scavenging activity and 81.4 % ferric reducing activity. The lower IC50 value indicates a stronger ability of the NCs to act as an effective scavenger of free radicals. The IC50 values of AgNP@CD NCs of the present study were represented in Table 2 .

Oxidative stress can lead to damage the cells, proteins, and DNA, contributing to various diseases and aging processes. Antioxidants are molecules that counteract oxidative stress by neutralizing ROS or preventing their formation. They work by donating electrons to ROS, thereby stabilizing them and reducing their damaging effects on cells. Carbon dots are carbon-based nanoparticles with unique optical and electronic properties [45]. They can act as antioxidants by scavenging ROS due to their surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, which have electron-donating properties. AgNPs themselves can exhibit antioxidant properties by scavenging ROS through their surface chemistry. Additionally, AgNPs can release silver ions (Ag+), which can act as antioxidants by scavenging ROS or by modulating antioxidant enzyme activity within cells. However, it’s important to note that AgNPs can also induce oxidative stress in microorganisms, contributing to their antimicrobial activity [46]. The efficient anti-oxidant property in the present study might be due to the synergistic effect of carbon dots and AgNPs.

The synthesized AgNP@CD NCs of the present study successfully inhibited the growth of both Vibrio species and neutralized free radicals in in vitro conditions. However, the current evaluation level is not sufficient for applying these NCs in real time. Hence, further studies are required to understand the optimal dosage level, pharmacokinetics, safety profile, and biodistribution of these NCs in in vivo animal models. Metabolomics and genomic approaches can monitor the long-term stability and mechanistic insights of these NCs in in vivo conditions.

Several earlier studies used a range of physical, chemical, and biological techniques to evaluate the optical, photocatalytic, and antimicrobial properties of ZnO [47], Ag-TiO2 [48], FeONPs [49], SnO2 [50], NiO [51], and Eu3+-doped Ba2Zn7F18 [52] etc., nanomaterials. Photoluminescence is a key property of CDs, and doping the CDs with various metal oxides can enhance their optical properties, which are useful in optoelectronics and biomedical fields. CD-doped nanomaterials have a diverse surface functionalization and ultrafine size, making them highly effective for photocatalysis applications and able to cross microbial membranes, resulting in microbial death.

Table 3 describes the previous studies reported on the synthesis of AgNP@CD nanocomposites and various precursors used for the synthesis of CDs. These studies reported the efficacy and applications of AgNP@CD nanocomposites in broad scientific fields, such as sensing of hazardous chemicals and ions, catalytic reduction of effluents, and pharmacological applications. A limited number of studies were carried out to evaluate the antimicrobial and antioxidant propertsies of AgNP@CD NCs, and the present study showed that plant-derived CDs are effective reducing agents for the synthesis of AgNPs and improving their efficiency for biomedical applications.

The successful synthesis of green AgNP@CD NCs was achieved using CDs derived from Aegle marmelos and Euphorbia hitra leaves, which was further confirmed by observing optical properties such as absorbance spectrum, surface plasmon resonance, surface functional groups, and phase analysis. Morphological properties revealed that most particles in both samples are uniform and spherically shaped. The size distribution range of AM AgNP@CDs revealed was 0–30 nm, with an average particle size diameter of 8.51 nm, which is significantly smaller than the particle size diameter of AgNPs synthesized using plat extracts. The synthesized both NCs showed effective inhibition of Vp and Vh growth with the lowest MIC of 3.9 μg mL-1 on Vp by AM AgNP@CDNCs. Efficient antioxidant property was also observed on reduction of nitrogen free radical and ferric ions.This demonstrates that the synthesis of AgNP@CD NCs is simple, economic, ecofriendly, and reproducible, and it also confirms that the smaller AgNPs show effective antimicrobial properties because of their easy penetration into the microbial membranes.However, the current study is only limited to the in vitro studies, further work must be carried out to understand the alteration of microbial metabolomics and toxicity towards the vibrio infected organism. The effect on normal flora of cultured organism, soil and water microbiota should be established.
